thread.title_full: The Roundhouse Report: Politics in New Mexico | News | santafenewmexican.com

author: Daniel J. Chacón dchacon@sfnewmexican.com
title: The Roundhouse Report: Politics in New Mexico | News | santafenewmexican.com
text:
MLG digs in heels on Trump's mass deportation plans
Subscribers of The New Mexican heard the news first.
The governor told the newspaper Nov. 15 [her administration would "resist"](https://www.santafenewmexican.com/news/local_news/lujan-grisham-vows-to-resist-trumps-mass-deportation-plans-in-new-mexico/article_e88e4978-a111-11ef-973c-0bef10af2e10.html) President-elect Donald Trump's mass deportation plans, though she pointed out New Mexico is already cooperating with efforts to hold people accountable if they are in the country illegally and committing crimes.
In [an interview Thursday](https://www.msnbc.com/morning-joe/watch/-we-are-not-going-to-cooperate-new-mexico-s-governor-pushes-back-on-deportation-plans-225092165658) on MSNBC's Morning Joe, Gov. Michelle Lujan Grisham reiterated her position.
Former White House press secretary Jen Psaki noted Lujan Grisham was on record saying she wasn't going to help with Trump's "aspirations to do mass deportations" and asked what she could specifically do to stop Trump's efforts.
In response, Lujan Grisham said it's "unlawful" to use the military for mass deportations.
"Disrupting family status and the economy, inhumane, cruel, unfair, discriminate policies that are illegal on their face, we are not going to cooperate in any way in that effort," she said.
Governor goes, goes, goes
Lujan Grisham's caffeine intake is legendary, and it's no wonder.
Tuesday evening, she flew to Dallas for a quick overnight stay.
Lujan Grisham woke up "very early" Wednesday morning and flew to Washington, D.C., to meet with the administrator of the Centers for Medicaid and Medicare Services, spokesperson Michael Coleman wrote in an email. She then met with New Mexico Sen. Ben Ray Luján, [did an interview on CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/video/new-mexico-governor-how-democrats-move-forward-after-harris-loss/) with Major Garrett, delivered a speech to the National Caregivers Association and then attended a private dinner with other panelists participating in the The Washington Post's Global Women’s Summit.
On Thursday, Lujan Grisham appeared on Morning Joe before meeting with former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi in her office on Capitol Hill. She then participated in a panel at the Global Women's Summit alongside two other Democratic women governors — Maura Healey of Massachusetts and Laura Kelly of Kansas — before flying back to New Mexico.
"It was a very busy and highly productive trip to the nation’s capital, but she is eager to get home to New Mexico," Coleman wrote, adding Lujan Grisham would be back in the office Friday.
Stansbury calls Trump's Cabinet picks 'dangerous'
The governor wasn't the only New Mexican in the national spotlight.
U.S. Rep. Melanie Stansbury of the state's 1st Congressional District [appeared Tuesday on MSNBC](https://x.com/Rep_Stansbury/status/1858908043171557863) and continued to raise alarms about Trump's Cabinet picks. In a virtual news briefing with New Mexico news media the week before, Stansbury called Trump's nominees "bizarre" and said they offered a "grim outlook" for the nation.
Asked about a report suggesting the Trump transition team skipped standard vetting protocols, including FBI background checks on some of the nominees, Stansbury said she was "absolutely" concerned about the consequences for national security.
"That is part of why Donald Trump is trying to get recess appointments," she said. "What he is essentially trying to do is to rewrite the checks and balances that Congress has on the executive branch, which is exactly what they did with the [U.S.] Supreme Court."
Stansbury said Trump "stacked" the nation's high court, which gave him "unlimited executive power."
"He is now trying to put political pressure on the Senate to basically give a green light to anyone he wants to put in, whether they're qualified, whether they're dangerous, so that they can go in and purge the federal government and carry out his agenda," she said.
Vasquez advocates for ranchers
U.S. Rep. Gabe Vasquez of New Mexico's 2nd Congressional District was [also in the spotlight](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=86kSa_K50sc) Tuesday but before a much, much smaller audience.
Fresh off winning a second term in a hard-fought race against Republican Yvette Herrell, Vasquez appeared before the House Committee on Natural Resources to advocate for a bipartisan bill that aims to reduce unnecessary regulations on ranchers.
"The current federal permitting process for minor range improvements is excessively burdensome and needs reform," he said. "Growers who simply need to repair a fence or improve their waters are waiting months, sometimes even years, for new permit approvals."
Vasquez emphasized his proposed Ranching Without Red Tape Act, which Republican U.S. Sen. John Barrasso is spearheading in the Senate, isn't about taking shortcuts or sacrificing environmental oversight.
"Ranchers are already deeply invested in protecting the lands that they rely on" he said. "Ranchers and environmentalists do not have to be at odds with each other on this issue."
